item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 4339 doesn't physically effect the real world. It wouldn't nullify 682's properties, it would just make everyone think its properties were nullified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Weird they don't want to test it more. Like saying, oh, I don't know, something like "SCP-682 doesn't have any regenerative properties."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: #JusticeForJuniorResearcherAdams

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So simple yet so entertaining. There aren't any baggage or fat on this article. It gets straight to the point, keeps you on track, and tells a complete story all under four minutes of reading.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this but some of the experiment logs felt inconsistent with how the foundation behaves, such as using researchers and doctors to write things in it, and the last thing about declassification of him made sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very cool! Thanks for doing this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-4339 has been translated to Japanese.
Thanks for all the support on my first scip! I really appreciate it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: O5-14 wasn't actually an O5. It was Dr. Archibald, who used the pen to become O5-14.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent execution of a simple idea. Feels very much like a Series 1 or 2 skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean I guess what Adams did was net good, but they definitely shouldn't have let their personal beliefs get involved in their work, especially with regards to an anomaly this powerful. In my personal opinion, any changes like that should be procedurally handled to minimize harm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP acting responsible and top of things. Also, good on Adams for helping pass a new Amendment and how the SCP decided "wait a minute, maybe we should think things through" and decided to explain the new Amendment rather than try and get rid of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. This is just amazing. You took the boring "pen that writes things into reality" cliché, flipped it on its head, and executed it in the best way possible. +1
Also, this line gets me every time

Experiment 4339-11
Procedure: D-91386 was told to say "my name is D-88111".
Results: D-88111's identification was changed.
Exciting times! I'm glad people enjoy this work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I get the general idea, but there seems to be an unnecessary level of obfuscation both on the description and the logs which is just confusing me as to what's actually going on. If the foundation have figured out a problem with the level of access granted to the pen and with the trustworthiness of its researchers and experimental personnel, to the extent that it's caused an O5 to be removed from office (correctly? otherwise?) one would expect that the events leading up to it would be covered in better detail, with clear redactions where you don't want to name names or whatever rather than the data simply being omitted.

It's interesting, and there's a clever idea here, particularly in that it seems to be more of a semantic threat than an actual reality altering one (ie it changes how people perceive things, and we've seen with e.g. The Forest Where Names Have Power that this can really, really mess things up, even within a Foundation investigation that's otherwise worked out that there's *something* wrong)… but because I reach the end of it feeling baffled, and that I'm baffled by deliberately incomplete writing, it's a no-vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Indeed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find the recovery story of the messianic schizophrenic extremely cliché and unnecessary

I can definitely understand that. My main goals with the discovery was to make something that (1) made more sense than the original one1 and (2) also explained why the previous possessor wouldn't also take advantage of it the artifact and destroy/significantly alter the world. Mentally ill person seemed like a reasonable out.

Isn’t it weird that access to it wasn’t forbidden to people who weren’t absolutely dependable

I was originally going to try for an ending that banked more on the idea that "power corrupts", but it didn't really end up that way. I'll probably revisit the ending after a few months of a break from this thing and can reapproach it with a fresh mind.

Though,

it had the power to make a company appear out of nowhere

It only created a legal entity with a given name. It doesn't affect the physical world, it couldn't give that company a bunch of employees and an office building.

Footnotes
1. When the SCP was still in the sandbox.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-4339 exhibits its anomalous effects when written with held.
When the current possessor makes a statement about the world

It triggers when you write with it or when you hold it. (Note that to write with a pen, you must also be holding it)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find the recovery story of the messianic schizophrenic extremely cliché and unnecessary. Also, by the time the research staff used the pen for their own purposes, the Foundation was already suspecting that it had the power to make a company appear out of nowhere - Isn’t it weird that access to it wasn’t forbidden to people who weren’t absolutely dependable? These things worsen what is otherwise a good article :-/

On the other hand, the presentation of the anomaly, the slow buildup of the test logs, and the exploration of the implications are all really good. The reading was engaging and interesting. You've taken a simple concept and executed it surprisingly well, take your +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So the pen is activated just by holding it or by writing with it? I can't tell from the article.

item.title: Re: Author post
item.content:encoded: this was an awesome read. clever idea very well executed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A pen that is definitely mightier than the sword!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know, right? It could have easily been just another OP object, but instead it was legitimately enjoyable. Kudos to you, author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting implications saved this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a well-executed version of something I've just seen too many times to want to upvote. So, neutral vote. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The execution of this is perfect. The Foundation is presented as the competent organization they are, the inner logic flows nicely along the experiment logs, and the twist is so smooth I almost didn't notice it.

Excellent work, author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting for competent Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’d normally downvote a “pen that alters reality,” but the way it’s presented here feels relatively fresh and interesting.

+1

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded:
Thanks a lot to wctaiwan, Oakfeather, mayoculpa, DarkStuff, fieldstone, and apn123 for looking at my drafts. The drafts and critiques forum thread doesn't have all of the reviews, as some were conducted over IRC and were not transcribed.

I drew a lot of inspiration from "What colour are your bits?" and "Why humans run the world | Yuval Noah Harari". The immaterial world humans mutually create and live in is very powerful despite its artificiality, and I hoped to give a taste of the danger that lies in being able to hijack it.

The image was an original creation by me. It is available under the same Creative Commons terms as the text.
